insights: Cost Reduction goals across all departments exhibit systematically extended durations—ranging from 256.9 to 284.6 days by department—confirming the phenomenon is category-wide, not Finance-specific.

Cost Reduction goal duration increases linearly with start month (r=0.997), rising by 18.3 days per month and varying from 186.6 days in Q1 to 354.4 days in Q4—a 167.8-day gap driven entirely by fiscal/budget cycle misalignment.

Seven alternative drivers—including department, priority, state, owner specialization, text length, complexity, and metric type—are statistically refuted (all p > 0.05, negligible effect sizes), confirming fiscal timing as the sole dominant root cause.

Shifting Cost Reduction goal initiation from Q4 to Q1 would reduce mean duration by 76.4 days (29.1%), directly accelerating goal closure without requiring changes to ownership, prioritization, or description practices.

The near-perfect temporal correlation (r=0.997) and absence of any comparable pattern in non-Cost-Reduction categories (r=0.076, p=0.17) indicate that budget cycle dependency is intrinsic to Cost Reduction goal execution logic, not a general operational artifact.

Long-duration Cost Reduction goals (>Q75) are overwhelmingly concentrated in Q4 (85%), while short-duration goals (<Q25) occur exclusively in Q1—revealing a high-risk initiation window that creates predictable processing backlogs and delays.
summary: The extended durations of Cost Reduction goals are not attributable to Finance department practices, owner specialization, priority settings, or textual complexity—but are instead driven almost exclusively by misalignment with fiscal and budget cycles: duration increases monotonically with start month (r=0.997), from 186.6 days in Q1 to 354.4 days in Q4. This pattern holds across all departments and is absent in all other goal categories, confirming it as a structural feature of Cost Reduction goal execution. As a result, Q4-initiated goals create predictable bottlenecks, with 85% of long-duration cases originating in that quarter. Shifting initiation to Q1 would yield a 29.1% reduction in mean duration—76.4 days—representing the highest-leverage, lowest-effort improvement opportunity. The next focus area should be embedding fiscal calendar alignment into goal intake governance, including automated start-date validation and Q1 initiation incentives for Cost Reduction goals.
